facts.label: BORN LIKE DEVILS
facts.story: In 1905, a Dutch reporter watched Belgium's players and wrote that they 'worked like devils.' The nickname stuck the following year when Léopold FC manager Pierre Walckiers, taken by the same fighting spirit and the team's red jerseys, began calling them the Red Devils after three straight wins. More than a century later, the name and the shirt color have never changed.

facts.label: THE WHITE DEVILS
facts.story: For most of the 1970s, Belgium abandoned red altogether. An all-white kit that had started as an away option became their regular home strip from 1970 through 1979. Wearing white, Belgium won their first-ever World Cup match, 3–0 over El Salvador in 1970, and made their first European Championship appearance in 1972 — briefly earning the nickname 'White Devils' before red reclaimed the shirt.

1930.headline: The First Whistle Ever
1930.story: Belgium and the United States kicked off what is credited as the first-ever World Cup match on 13 July 1930, a contest Belgium lost 3–0. Referee John Langenus, a Belgian, went on to officiate the tournament's final. A second scoreless defeat to Paraguay sent the Red Devils home from the inaugural tournament without a goal to their name.

annotations.label: SCORELESS IN URUGUAY
annotations.story: Belgium became a footnote in history on 13 July 1930, kicking off the very first World Cup match and losing 3–0 to the United States. Four days later Paraguay beat them 1–0. Two matches, zero goals scored — the Red Devils' entire opening World Cup summed up in a single blank scoreline.

annotations.label: THE LACED COLLAR
annotations.story: Belgium's 1930 shirt was heavy cotton, dyed deep red, buttoned at a laced V-collar with no crest, no sponsor, no numbers stitched to the back for most of the tournament. It was the same plain jersey clubs across Europe wore that decade — functional wool-blend cloth built for mud, not television cameras.

annotations.label: FIFTEEN DAYS AT SEA
annotations.story: To reach Uruguay, Belgium's squad boarded the liner Conte Verde in Barcelona, sharing the ship with France, Romania, three referees, FIFA president Jules Rimet, and the World Cup trophy itself. Belgian vice-president Rodolphe Seeldrayers had helped talk Europe into sending anyone at all — the fifteen-day crossing nearly outlasted the tournament.

1930.homeColors: red shirt, white shorts, red socks

1934.headline: Gone After One Match
1934.story: With no group stage in 1934, one loss meant elimination. Germany overwhelmed Belgium 5–2 in the tournament's single-elimination opening round, the Red Devils' heaviest-scoring World Cup defeat to that point. It was a quick, forgettable trip to Italy that ended before the second week began.

annotations.label: ONE MATCH, DONE
annotations.story: There was no group stage in 1934 — one loss and you sailed home. Germany made sure of it early, racing to a 5–2 win that stood as Belgium's heaviest World Cup defeat for decades. Two goals from Bernard Voorhoof were the only consolation in a ruthless single-elimination format.

annotations.label: A FACELESS RED SHIRT
annotations.story: FIFA hadn't yet mandated squad numbers for the 1934 tournament, so Belgium's players took the field in identical unmarked red shirts, white shorts, and red socks — indistinguishable to spectators beyond a program sheet. The look wouldn't change for another World Cup; individual shirt numbers were still years away.

annotations.label: A QUALIFICATION FLUKE
annotations.story: Belgium actually lost their head-to-head qualifier to the Netherlands 4–2 that April. They advanced anyway: the Dutch federation withdrew from the competition afterward, handing Belgium the European group spot on goal difference instead — a backdoor route to Italy that had nothing to do with beating anyone.

1934.homeColors: red shirt, white shorts, red socks

1938.headline: France Ends It Fast
1938.story: France needed just one match to see off Belgium, winning 1–3 in the tournament's opening round. It was Belgium's second straight single-match exit under the era's ruthless knockout format, a quiet World Cup that did little to ease the disappointment of 1934.

annotations.label: PARIS ENDS IT FAST
annotations.story: Same format, same result: one match, one elimination. France needed only ninety minutes at the Stade Olympique Yves-du-Manoir to beat Belgium 3–1, continuing a run of first-round World Cup exits that had defined the Red Devils since their 1930 debut. No team had yet shown Belgium a second match.

annotations.label: STITCHED FOR AMATEURS
annotations.story: Belgium's 1938 squad were still largely amateurs — clerks, students, tradesmen — who wore the same unadorned red jersey and knee-length white shorts their predecessors had worn in 1930 and 1934. No sponsor patches existed yet in international football; the crest was the only mark separating the shirt from a training top.

annotations.label: THE LAST CUP FOR 16 YEARS
annotations.story: Nobody in Paris knew it yet, but 1938 would be the final World Cup before a sixteen-year gap. Within eighteen months, Belgium itself would be invaded and occupied by Germany — the same opponent that had eliminated the Red Devils from the previous tournament. Football in Europe simply stopped.

1938.homeColors: red shirt, white shorts, red socks

1954.headline: Wildest Draw at Basel
1954.story: Ivor Broadis and Nat Lofthouse built England a 3–1 lead early in the second half, but Belgium clawed back twice to force the first extra-time period of an England World Cup match, Jimmy Dickinson's own header sealing a wild 4–4 draw. Italy shut the door 1–4 four days later, ending the run.

annotations.label: EIGHT GOALS IN BASEL
annotations.story: England led 3–1 with barely half an hour gone, but Belgium clawed level twice, forcing the first extra-time period of an England World Cup match. Jimmy Dickinson's own header in the 94th minute finally settled it at 4–4 — still one of the wildest single matches Belgium has ever played.

annotations.label: AN ENGLISHMAN IN CHARGE
annotations.story: Belgium's tidy red kit in Switzerland was overseen from the touchline by Doug Livingstone, an Englishman who drilled the Red Devils into a 3–2–5 'WM' shape — the same system England themselves used. It made the 4–4 classic against England, tactically, a mirror match between two sides speaking the same language.

annotations.label: BACK AFTER TWENTY YEARS
annotations.story: Belgium hadn't played a World Cup since 1938. War had cancelled the 1942 and 1946 tournaments outright, and Belgium skipped the first postwar edition in 1950 altogether. Switzerland 1954 marked their return to the world stage — the first World Cup broadcast, in part, on live television.

1954.homeColors: red shirt, white shorts, red socks

1970.headline: White Devils, First Win
1970.story: Playing in all-white for the first time, Belgium beat El Salvador 3–0 for their first-ever World Cup victory, forty years after their debut. The good feeling didn't last: the Soviet Union routed them 4–1 and hosts Mexico added a 1–0 defeat, sending the newly christened 'White Devils' out in the group stage.

annotations.label: FORTY YEARS FOR ONE WIN
annotations.story: Four decades and eight matches after their 1930 debut, Belgium finally won a World Cup game — 3–0 over El Salvador, with two goals from Wilfried Van Moer. The Soviet Union crushed the feeling four days later, 4–1, and a single Mexican goal sent the Red Devils home regardless.

annotations.label: THE FIRST ALL-WHITE KIT
annotations.story: For the first time in their history, Belgium walked out in all white — shirt, shorts, and socks — a strip that started as an away option and became their World Cup identity for the entire decade. Journalists needed a new nickname fast; 'White Devils' stuck almost as quickly as the jersey change did.

annotations.label: HIGH ALTITUDE, NEW CAMERAS
annotations.story: Mexico 1970 was the first World Cup broadcast in color worldwide and the first to allow substitutions and yellow and red cards. Belgium's squad, like every European team, also had to adjust to Mexico City and Puebla's thin air — a physical toll that hit visiting sides much harder than hosts.

1970.homeColors: white shirt, white shorts, white socks (the all-white 'White Devils' kit, worn as home strip 1970–1979)

1982.headline: Vandenbergh Stuns the Champions
1982.story: Erwin Vandenbergh's goal beat defending champions Argentina 1–0 in the tournament opener at Camp Nou, one of Belgium's most celebrated World Cup wins. A second-round hat-trick from Zbigniew Boniek and a Soviet Union defeat followed, but Belgium had already delivered its statement result before exiting.

annotations.label: THE OPENER THAT STUNNED SPAIN
annotations.story: Belgium had never beaten a reigning World Cup champion. Erwin Vandenbergh's 62nd-minute strike at a packed Camp Nou changed that, sinking defending champions Argentina 1–0 in the tournament's very first match. Diego Maradona left the pitch that night having lost his World Cup debut to unfancied Belgium.

annotations.label: ADIDAS TAKES OVER
annotations.story: By 1982, Adidas had taken over Belgium's kit contract, adding the brand's signature three stripes down each sleeve of the familiar red shirt. It was a small design change with a big symbolic one: Belgium's shirt now looked like the rest of Europe's biggest football nations, all dressed by the same German company.

annotations.label: A BIGGER WORLD CUP
annotations.story: 1982 was the first World Cup expanded to 24 teams, up from 16, opening the door to smaller footballing nations. Belgium's opening upset over Argentina became the template underdog story for the new format — proof the bigger tournament could still produce shocks nobody saw coming from outside the traditional powers.

1982.homeColors: red shirt, white shorts, red socks (Adidas)

1986.headline: Fourth Place, Forever Famous
1986.story: Belgium survived the group stage, then stunned the Soviet Union 4–3 in extra time despite an Igor Belanov hat-trick, and outlasted Spain on penalties. Diego Maradona scored twice in a 2–0 semi-final loss to eventual champions Argentina, and France won the third-place match 4–2 after extra time — still Belgium's best World Cup finish for 32 years.
1986.homeColors: red shirt, white shorts, red socks (Adidas, three-stripe trim)

1990.headline: Platt's Volley in Injury Time
1990.story: Wins over South Korea and Uruguay carried Belgium through the group, but a narrow loss to Spain cost them top spot. Enzo Scifo twice hit the woodwork against England in the Round of 16, and with penalties looming, David Platt's swiveling last-minute volley in extra time ended Belgium's tournament instead.
1990.homeColors: red shirt, white shorts, white socks (Adidas)

1994.headline: The Penalty That Wasn't
1994.story: Belgium beat Morocco and the Netherlands 1–0 apiece to reach the second round, where defending champions Germany won 3–2. Referee Kurt Röthlisberger waved away a clear penalty after Thomas Helmer bundled over Josip Weber in the box — Röthlisberger was sent home by FIFA after the match, but the result stood.
1994.homeColors: red shirt, white shorts, red socks (Diadora)

1998.headline: Unbeaten, Still Eliminated
1998.story: Three straight draws — against the Netherlands, Mexico, and South Korea — made Belgium one of only three teams at the tournament never to lose, alongside champions France and Italy. It wasn't enough to escape the group. Enzo Scifo and Franky Van der Elst became the first Belgians capped at four World Cups.
1998.homeColors: red shirt, white shorts, red socks (Diadora)

2002.headline: The Disallowed Goal
2002.story: Marc Wilmots scored in all three group matches, including the decisive 3–2 win over Russia that sent Belgium through. In the Round of 16 against eventual champions Brazil, referee Peter Prendergast chalked off a Wilmots header for a phantom foul before Brazil won 2–0. Belgium still walked away with the tournament's Fair Play award.
2002.homeColors: red shirt, white shorts, red socks (Nike)

2014.headline: Golden Generation's First Bow
2014.story: A new generation of Belgian stars won all three group games by a single goal each, then needed extra time to beat the United States in the Round of 16 behind Kevin De Bruyne and Romelu Lukaku. Gonzalo Higuaín's early strike ended the run in the quarter-final against Argentina, a first glimpse of what was coming.
2014.homeColors: red shirt, white shorts, red socks (Burrda)

2018.headline: Comeback in Kazan, Bronze in Petersburg
2018.story: Down 0–2 to Japan in the Round of 16, Belgium scored three times in the final 21 minutes, Nacer Chadli's stoppage-time strike completing the greatest comeback of the tournament. Kevin De Bruyne and Marouane Fellaini toppled Brazil in the quarter-final before France's Samuel Umtiti header ended the run in the semi. Belgium beat England again for third place — the best finish in Belgian history.
2018.homeColors: red shirt with diamond-pattern flag graphic on the chest, white shorts, red socks (Adidas, inspired by the Euro 1994 kit)
2018.awayColors: yellow shirt with red and black trim (Belgium's first-ever yellow kit, inspired by their 1984 goalkeeper jersey)

2022.headline: The Golden Generation's Last Stand
2022.story: Ranked second in the world and considered a serious title contender, Belgium instead lost 0–2 to Morocco and drew 0–0 with Croatia, crashing out in the group stage. It was a stunning collapse for the aging core that had reached the semi-final four years earlier, and the last World Cup shared by Belgium's golden generation together.
2022.homeColors: red shirt, black shorts, red socks (Adidas)
2022.awayColors: black shirt with red and yellow trim
